coredata.dc:description: As one of the major public health problems, cancers seriously threaten the human health. Among them, lung cancer is considered to be one of the most life-threatening malignancies. Therefore, developing early diagnosis technology and timely treatment for lung cancer is urgent. Recent research has witnessed that measuring changes of biomarkers expressed in lung cancer has practical significance. Meanwhile, we note that bioimaging with organic fluorescent probes plays an important role for its high sensitivity, real-time analysis and simplicity of operation. In the past years, kinds of organic fluorescent probes targeting lung cancer related biomarker have been developed. Herein, we summarize the research progress of organic fluorescent probes for the detection of lung cancer related biomarkers in this review, along with their design principle, luminescence mechanism and bioimaging application. Additionally, we put forward some challenges and future prospects from our perspective.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the major public health problems, cancers seriously threaten the human health. Among them, lung cancer is considered to be one of the most life-threatening malignancies. Therefore, developing early diagnosis technology and timely treatment for lung cancer is urgent. Recent research has witnessed that measuring changes of biomarkers expressed in lung cancer has practical significance. Meanwhile, we note that bioimaging with organic fluorescent probes plays an important role for its high sensitivity, real-time analysis and simplicity of operation. In the past years, kinds of organic fluorescent probes targeting lung cancer related biomarker have been developed. Herein, we summarize the research progress of organic fluorescent probes for the detection of lung cancer related biomarkers in this review, along with their design principle, luminescence mechanism and bioimaging application. Additionally, we put forward some challenges and future prospects from our perspective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lung cancer is one of the leading causes of death, accounting for 18.0% of all cancer deaths [1,2]. Once detected, most lung cancer patients will be diagnosed as advanced, and the 5-year survival rate after diagnosis is only 10–20% [3–5]. Due to the lack of early diagnosis technology, lung cancer is characterized by the high mortality and the low survival rate [6,7]. Therefore, significant efforts have been made to find suitable techniques and methods for early diagnosis of lung cancer in the past years.

As endogenous molecules differentially expressed in cancer cells, biomarkers could be used to distinguish normal cells from cancer cells. Measuring changes of biomarkers and understanding the dynamics of the disease process can assist the diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Therefore, the development of sensitive and reliable assays is of great value for the early diagnosis of lung cancer [8–11]. Multiple studies have shown that abnormal enzyme levels are considered to be an important signal for disease diagnosis [12,13]. In cancer diagnosis, bioimaging with organic fluorescent probes plays an important role for its high sensitivity, real-time detection and simplicity of operation [14–18]. In organic fluorescent probes, the receptor is the fluorophore unit that determines the sensitivity of probes, and the reportor is the recognition unit that determines the selectivity of probes. To date, kinds of organic fluorescent probes targeting lung cancer related biomarker have been developed, and some of them have been identified as promising probes for tumour selective visualization [19–22]. Through the careful literature investigation, we note that there are some main lung cancer related biomarkers, such as NAD(P)H: quinone acceptor oxidoreductase 1 (NQO1), Alkaline phosphatase (ALP), Carboxylesterases (CEs) and others. And for these lung cancer related biomarkers, the corresponding organic fluorescent probes also have their specific recognition units. Moreover, their luminescence mechanism includes the following types: Intramolecular Charge Transfer (ICT), Photo-induced Electron Transfer (PET), Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Excited Intramolecular Proton Transfer (ESIPT) and Aggregation-Induced Emission (AIE). Herein, as part of a program that we have been focusing on [23–25], we will deliver an overview of organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection, covering the design principle, the luminescence mechanism, the bioimaging application and the further development (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection and bioimaging application

ce:section.ce:para: NAD(P)H quinone oxidoreductase-1 (NQO1) is a protective enzyme against cellular oxidative stress [26], which is considered a tumor biomarker due to its abnormally high expression in various solid tumors. Given that NQO1 is an important therapeutic cancer target, the NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes have great potential application. in the cancer diagnosis and the enzyme-related biology study [27]. As shown in Table 1 , NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes are classified according to the luminescence mechanism, detection approach, linear range, detection limit, quantum yield, cell viability and bioimaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Organic fluorescent probes based on ICT are composed of the electron-withdrawing group and the electron-donating group, which are attached to fluorophores and accompanied by intramolecular electron transfer via π-bonds [44]. When it is excited by light to an excited state, the charge is transferred from the electrondonating group to the electron-withdrawing group, leading to changes in dipole moment and giving rise to the fluorescent changes. Based on this design principle, a variety of NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism have been developed in recent years (Fig. 2 ) [28–38].

In 2019, Zhu and colleagues designed a fluorescent probe (TCF–OH–QPA) for the detection of hNQO1, which contained a recognition group (quinone propionic acid, QPA) and a chromophore (2-dicyanomethylene-3-cyano-4,5,5-trimethyl-2,5-dihydrofuran derivative, TCF-OH) [31]. In the presence of hNQO1, the probe is reduced to trimethylhydroquinone, followed by the formation of a lactone structure and the release of the fluorophore TCF-OH, which causes the color of the solution to change from yellow to red (Fig. 3 a). The cytotoxicity of TCF–OH–QPA on MDA-MB-231 cells was measured using the CCK-8 assay, and its cell survival rate was about 83% when the concentration of TCF–OH–QPA was 14 mM, indicating its low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Furthermore, they also carried out intracellular hNQO1 imaging experiments in MDA-MB-231 cells, and the results indicated that the probe could be applied in early diagnosis of cancer (Fig. 3b).

In 2020, Han et al. developed two NQO1-activated fluorescent probes (Q3PA-4HA and Q3PA-2 ME) via a covalent assembly strategy with trimethyl lock quinone propionic acid (Q3PA) as the recognition group and the coumarin precursor 2-cyano-3-(4 (diethylamino)2-hydroxyphenyl) acrylic acid as the fluorescent group (Fig. 3c) [32]. The linear range of Q3PA-4HA for hNQO1 detection was 0.125–4 μg/mL, while the linear range of Q3PA-2 ME for hNQO1 detection was 0.25–4 μg/mL. These two probes show weak fluorescence when excited at 435 nm. When they were incubated with NQO1 and NADH, a distinct emission peak at 490 nm was observed at the same excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity increased by nearly 7-10-fold. They are also rapidly recognized and reduced by NQO1 and are not disturbed by NADH and other biologically relevant substances, showing their good selectivity and sensitivity. Owing to their good water solubility, stability and low toxicity to cells, they were successfully used for fluorescence imaging in living cells. Overall, these two NQO1-activatable probes have good application prospects for early clinical diagnosis and drug efficacy evaluation in preclinical studies.

Near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) imaging can be used to detect hNQO1 activity for its highly sensitive, non-invasive and rapid. But is not suitable for longitudinal imaging in vivo due to its limited penetration depth [45,46]. Photoacoustic (PA) imaging is an effective complement to NIRF imaging, enabling deep tissue penetration and improving the sensitivity and reliability of detection. In 2022, Zhang et al. synthesized an activatable hemi-anthocyanine dye-based probe (LET-10) for NIRF/PA imaging, affording the detection limit in 0.21 μg/mL and 0.31 μg/mL, respectively (Fig. 3d) [37]. In this study, the linear range of the probe is 0–5 μg/mL. While catalyzed by hNQO1, the quinone propionic of LET-10 was cleaved, and then the signal of LET-10 was enhanced. MTT assay revealed that when LET-10 (80 μM) incubated with A549 cells for 24 h, the cell viability is greater than 80%. Based on its excellent biocompatibility, the probe has been successfully applied to the real-time detection of hNQO1 via dual NIRF/PA imaging in A549 cells (Fig. 3e), indicating that it has great application prospects in the early evaluation of diseases with the overexpression of hNQO1 in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the PET mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: PET is the process of electron transfer between the electron donor and electron acceptor in the excited state after being excited by light. Recently, a significant progress has been made on NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the PET mechanism (Fig. 4 a) [39–43]. Most probes are “turn-on” type, affording the higher signal-to-noise ratio and the fluorescent imaging capability.

In 2019, Park's group developed a “turn-on” fluorescent probe (TPP-Naphthalimide, a naphthalimido-trimethyl lock quinone containing a triphenylphosphonium salt), which performed lower cytotoxicity and higher enzymatic efficiency (Fig. 4b) [40]. After addition of hNQO1, the TLQ group (trimethyl lock quinone) was cleaved and the naphthalimide was formed, giving a strong yellow fluorescence. Then, the cytotoxicity of probe was assessed in two different lung cancer cell lines (HNQO1-positive A549 and HNQO1-negative H596), displaying negligible cytotoxic effects in these two cells even after 24 h of incubation. Owing to its fairly biocompatibility, this probe could provide strong fluorescence images in hNQO1-overexpressing cancer cells (A549 cells). They found that A549 cells positive for HNQO1 treated with probe 1 showed significant green fluorescence, while relatively weak fluorescence was observed in H596 cells negative for HNQO1 (Fig. 4c).

In 2022, Zhang et al. synthesized an NIR fluorescent probe (S-QCy7-NQO1) (Fig. 4d) [43]. In this probe, Q3PA (trimethyl-locked quinone propionate) is an electron deficient trigger that generates the fluorescence burst via a PET process. When the catalytic reduction activity of NQO1 occurred, S-QCy7 was released from S-QCy7-NQO1. As an important response performance to NQO1, S-QCy7-NQO1 had a significantly faster response with a final quantum yield of 19.7%. Of note, it exhibited a significant Stokes shift of 105 nm and a 245-fold fluorescence enhancement at 695 nm once in contact with NQO1. Its cytotoxicity was determined by CCK-8 assay with above 95% of the cell viability in HT-29 and H596 cells, indicating that the probe had good biocompatibility and was suitable for the detection of NQO1 in living cells. Further research found that S-QCy7-NQO1 owned the better stability and fluorescence intensity in differentiating between NQO1-overexpressed tumor cells and normal cells, and was applied for high-contrast imaging of endogenous NQO1 in living cells and non-invasive visualization of NQO1 activity in vivo. The results revealed that this probe would be an effective and unprecedented tool for real-time monitoring of NQO1 activity in cells and in vivo, providing a new broad perspective for studying changes in NQO1 activity levels in NQO1-related diseases.

The FRET system usually contains pairs of matched fluorophores, which are defined as donor and acceptor fluorophores, respectively. The overlap between the emission of donor fluorophores and the excitation of receptor fluorophores is a necessary condition for FRET. In other words, when the matched fluorophores are close enough, the donor is excited to a higher electron energy state after absorbing photons of a certain frequency. Subsequently, the energy is transferred to the neighboring acceptor via the interaction of dipole, rather than back to the ground state [47]. In 2019, Zhang et al. developed a dual-biomarker-triggered fluorescence probe (Q3PA-NBD-naphthalimide) that were rationally constructed by two chemoselective triggering units (Q3PA and NBD units) and one naphthalimide fluorophore (Fig. 5 ) [42]. The Q3PA unit could switch off the fluorescence according to PET effect for the detection of hNQO1, while the NBD unit can quench the fluorescence through FRET effect for the detection of H2S. Prior to activation, the probe was non-fluorescent. In the presence of hNQO1 and H2S, it had a significantly enhanced fluorescence by dual activation (220-fold). But when the probe was treated with H2S alone, only a 34-fold fluorescence enhancement was observed, which was much lower than the response of hNQO1 and H2S together. Also with the hNQO1 treatment alone, its emission enhancement was negligible (2-fold). In this work, the cytotoxicity of Q3PA-NBD-naphthalimide was evaluated in HT29 cells using a MTT assay, which showed a low cytotoxicity with more than 80% of cell viability at 0–15 μM of the probe. Interestingly, this work proposed a proposed mechanism of the synergistic antioxidant effect of H2S and hNQO1 under oxidative stress. In this mechanism, hNQO1 was regulated by the Keap 1 (Kelch-like ECH-related protein 1)/Nrf2 (nuclear factor-erythroid 2 related factor 2)/ARE (antioxidant response element) pathway. Nrf2 protein level might respond to rapid increase in ROS, triggering expression of hNQO1 to inhibit free radical formation. At the same time, Nrf2 elevation could increase the expression of glutathione reductase (GSR), resulting in reducing GSSG to GSH. This GSH could participate in the glutathionylation of CBS under H2O2 to produce CBS-SG, which would make the biosynthesis of endogenous H2S more efficient. Overall, the probe exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and good biocompatibility, which was enough to differentiate activation levels in tumor cells.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is a glycoproteinase secreted by osteoblasts and found mainly in the cytoplasm [48,49]. Clinically, abnormal levels of ALP are associated with the development of many diseases, such as diabetes, bone disease, liver dysfunction and lung cancer [50–53]. As a malignant tumour, lung cancer can develop bone metastases at an advanced stage, and therefore monitoring ALP levels in a patient's serum is potentially valuable in the diagnosis of lung cancer. Considering that ALP concentration in human serum is ranging from 46 to 190 U/L, developing ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes is of particular importance [54–56]. As shown in Table 2 , ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes are classified according to the luminescence mechanism, detection approach, linear range, detection limit, quantum yield, cell viability and bioimaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, several ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism have been developed (Fig. 6 a) [57–64]. There are few NIR probes for animal ALP imaging. In 2017, Tan et al. designed a NIR probe (DHXP) that was consist of a fluorophore DHX and a phosphatase reaction site [60]. When ALP is present, the dephosphorylation of DHXP happens, resulting in an ICT effect and the “turn-on” of fluorescence (Fig. 6b). In this study, DHXP had a low detection limit of 0.07 U/L and the linear range in 0–100 U/L. By a standard MTT assay, the cytotoxicity of DHXP was assessed. It was found that the cell viability remained unchanged after incubation with 20 μM DHXP for 24 h, indicating that DHXP had good biocompatibility. And the fluorescence images of DHXP in HeLa cells and HEK 293 cells were conducted (Fig. 6c). Taken together, DHXP showed a good stability, high selectivity and low cytotoxicity, which could be used for bioimaging in HeLa cells as well as in mice.

Mono-aromatic molecules have advantages of structural stability, easy-preparation and low cost. In 2021, Tian's group characterized some mono-aromatic molecules' UV–Vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra [63]. It showed the intense fluorescence in the visible region, indicating that these mono-aromatic molecules characterised by intramolecular hydrogen bonding performed a greater red-shifted emission. In Fig. 6d, phosphorylated DHTA (PDHTA) underwent a phosphate cleavage and DHTA regeneration in the presence of ALP, accompanied by a significant change in the fluorescence intensity. The cytotoxicity of PDHTA on HeLa cells was studied by the standard MTT assay. The result showed its cell viability was higher than 90% after 24 h of incubation, suggesting its negligible cytotoxicity. Subsequently, fluorescence imaging of endogenous ALP and its inhibition in HeLa cancer cells were proceeded, and the results demonstrated the feasibility of PDHTA in monitoring endogenous ALP. According to these fluorescence images, the probe accumulated in the cytoplasm rather than in the nucleus. In short, this work holds a promise for the development of organic probes with tunable emission for fluorescence analysis and imaging via different intramolecular hydrogen bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: ESIPT is the transfer of hydrogen atoms (mainly from hydroxyl or amino groups) to nearby heteroatoms (mainly N, O or S). In recent years, a significant progress has been made in developing ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on ESIPT mechanism (Fig. 7 a) [65–71]. Mitochondria contain enzymes that catalyse protein synthesis and contain very high levels of ALP. Therefore, the detection of ALP activity in mitochondria is necessary. In 2017, Liu et al. developed a new solid-state fluorochrome (HTPQ) based on the ESIPT mechanism, which was utilized in the design of an enzyme-responsive probe (HTPQA) for the detection of ALP [68]. Once encountering with ALP, HTPQA was transferred to the molecule HTPQ, accompany by an obvious orange fluorescent signal through an ESIPT process (Fig. 7b). In this work, HPTQA could be used to detect ALP activity in osteosarcoma cells and other live cancer cells, thus showing a particular promise to reveal the role of ALP in physiological and pathological processes.

In 2019, Zhang's group synthesized an extremely water-soluble ratiometric fluorescent probe (HBTP-mito, a phosphorylated 2-(2′-hydroxyphenyl)-benzothiazole derivative) [69]. The probe was designed to detect ALP by an ESIPT process, and ALP could hydrolyze phosphate and release hydroxyl groups efficiently. The phosphate ester of HBTP-mito was hydrolyzed in response to ALP and the HBT fluorophore was released, followed by the activated ESIPT process with a huge wavelength shift and a quantum yield of 21% (Fig. 7c). Cytotoxicity was then investigated by the CCK8 assay, which showed that cell viability remained above 80% after 5 h of incubation with 2–50 μM of HBTP-Mito or HBT-Mito. HeLa cells showed good viability even when incubated with 10 μM HBTP-Mito or HBT-Mito for 24 h. This probe could also be used to monitor ALP in mitochondria, HeLa cells and A549 cell lines (Fig. 7d). In particular, the lower cytotoxicity makes it more useful for the detection of lung cancer biomarkers. In addition, HBTP-mito can also detect ALP activity in serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the AIE mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Meanwhile, we note that kinds of ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the AIE mechanism have been developed (Fig. 8 a) [72–82]. The phosphate group has a strong influence on the water solubility of ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes. AIE characterised probes do not fluoresce when present alone, but fluoresce strongly when present in an aggregated state or as a solid. Therefore, AIE probes have the advantage of long-lasting fidelity and are more suitable for biomarker detection. Owing to good luminescence property, easy structural modification and simple synthesis, Tetrastyrene (TPE) is usually used as an AIE fluorescent unit to construct AIE probes. In 2018, Zhang et al. designed a cationic AIE fluorescent probe (TPEQN-P) derived from TPE-QI via phosphorylation [78]. In the presence of ALP, TPEQN-P was converted to TPE-QI, along with an intense green fluorescence emitting due to a decreasing water solubility (Fig. 8b). In this study, the optimal detection condition for the probe was incubation with ALP for 1 h at 37 °C in 80% Tris-HCl buffer solution (10 mM, pH 9.0) containing 20% DMSO. And the detection limit of the probe was as low as 0.0077 U/L, and the linear range was 0–30 U/L. Meanwhile, TPEQN-P is not only suitable for the detection of ALP activity, but also available for the detection of serum samples.

In 2023, Lam et al. developed a versatile ALP-responsive probe (TPAPyP) with AIE and photosensitive properties [82]. In the enzymatic responsiveness activity of TPAPyP, its solution did not show luminescence in the absence of ALP. As ALP was added and the incubation time elapsed, the fluorescence peak at 540 nm concomitantly increased due to ALP-mediated hydrolysis of TPAPyP and formation of emissive TPAPy aggregates (Fig. 8c). In addition, due to the higher expression of ALP in cancer cells compared to normal cells, TPAPyP exhibited selective fluorescence in cancer cells, which was successfully used for differential cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 8d, it could be observed that the difference in fluorescence intensity between HeLa and MDCK cells was evident as the incubation time increased. Specifically, TPAPyP showed better performance in terms of total ROS generation efficiency upon exposure to white light irradiation, enabling it to perform well in photodynamic ablation of cancer cells. Since TPAPyP generates ROS upon typical enzymatic reactions, this work is related to the future application of cancer treatment through discriminative PDT, providing a strategy for designing specific tumor marker responsive photosensitizers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT-AIE mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To date, some ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT-AIE mechanism have been developed (Fig. 9 a) [83–86]. In 2019, Li and colleagues designed an AIE fluorescent probe (FAS-P) based on the ESIPT mechanism, which was consist of a phosphate group recognition unit and (E)-2-(((9H-fluoren-9-ylidene) hydrazono)methyl)phenyl dihydrogen phosphate [84]. With the continuous addition of ALP, the conversion from FAS-P to FAS was correspondingly accelerated and the fluorescence intensity was enhanced due the AIE characteristic in aqueous solution (Fig. 9b). In this study, FAS-P was extremely sensitive and selective for ALP with a linear range of 1–100 U/L and a detection limit as low as 0.6 U/L. To confirm whether FAS-P had the ability to be used for fluorescence imaging of ALP in living cells, HeLa cells were selected as a model and MTT assays were performed to assess cell viability in the presence of different concentrations of FAS-P and FAS. The results revealed that even at 10 μM, FAS-P and FAS showed negligible cytotoxicity. Owing to its real-time in situ monitoring of ALP, this work provided a promising AIE probe for the quantification of ALP in biological samples with potential clinical applications.

In 2021, Huang's group developed an ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probe (THP) based on AIE and ESIPT mechanism [86]. In the probe, the phosphoryl group was designed as a recognition unit, and 2-(benzo[d]thiazol-2-yl)-4-(1,4,5-triphenyl-1H-imidazole-2-yl)phenol was served as a fluorophore unit. In the absence of ALP, the probe exhibited blue fluorescence in DMSO solution. On the contrary, the phosphate of THP was hydrolysised in the presence of ALP, which performed a yellow fluorescence change at 550 nm (Fig. 9c). Next, the ability of the probe to quantitatively detect ALP at different concentrations and response times was investigated. The response of THP to ALP showed a good linear relationship with a limit of detection of 1.21228 U/L in the range of 0–200 U/L. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of THP on HeLa cells were evaluated by CCK-8 assay. After incubation with different concentrations of THP solution (0–40 μM) for 24 h, the viability of HeLa cells remained at a high level (above 80%), indicating that its cytotoxicity to the cultured cells was limited. According to control experiments with other substances, THP showed high selectivity for ALP. Meanwhile, THP was also available for live cell imaging, such as HeLa cells, A549 cells and Hek293 cells.

Carboxylesterases (CEs) are serine hydrolases arised from the liver, which have both CE1 and CE2 isoforms in humans. CEs are widely involved in the hydrolysis of endogenous and exogenous esters, amides and thioesters in vivo [87–92]. CEs are also involved in the growth, migration and invasion of malignant tumors [93], which are overexpressed in malignant tumors, such as pancreatic cancer, neuroblastoma [94], non-small cell lung cancer [95] and colorectal cancer [96]. Therefore, CEs can be used as a potential target for cancer diagnosis. Owing to their substrate specificity and structural similarity, the development of CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes is of great significance. As shown in Table 3 , CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes are classified according to the luminescence mechanism, detection approach, linear range, detection limit, quantum yield, cell viability and bioimaging application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Until now, several CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ICT mechanism have been developed (Fig. 10 a) [97–103]. Lysosomes are known as the digestive organs of eukaryotic cells and involved in various cellular metabolic regulatory processes. In 2022, Zhang et al. utilized indenone derivatives’ organoluminescent properties to synthesize a CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probe (Indenone-CE), which could achieve localization function in lysosomes and also be imaged in zebrafish [100]. In this probe, a morpholine unit was introduced to the indene-chalcone backbone as a lysosome-targetable group, and the acetyl group was served as an enzyme activity trigger. Indenone-CE itself performed weak fluorescence with a quantum yield of 15% using fluorescein (Φ = 0.95 in 0.1 M NaOH) as the reference. Once added CEs (Fig. 10b), a significant green fluorescent signal was observed, and the quantum yield was calculated as 70%. In cytotoxicity assay, Indenone-CE maintained the viability of C6 cells above 81%, indicating its relatively low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. In this work, Indenone-CE was capable of lysosomal localization and could be used for cellular and zebrafish imaging of endogenous CE.

In 2023, Li and colleagues introduced 4-bromomethyl-phenyl ester into DBPpy, giving rise to a CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probe (DBPpys) [101]. Acetyl group was taken as the recognition moiety of DBPpys, linking to the DBPpy. When adding CEs, the probe DBPpys released the fluorophore DBPpy with a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence response, which was localized in lipid droplets (LDs) with a colour change (Fig. 10c and d). In this study, they found that the fluorescence emission spectra in toluene and DMF performed a red-shifted pattern along with a polarity enhancement. DBPpys was facilitated by ICT mechanism from the phenazine donor (D) to the cyanopyridine electron acceptor (A), thus forming a large Stokes shift. More importantly, its in vitro detection limit was as low as 9.38 × 10−5 U/mL, which had potential advantages for cell imaging and CE evaluation. Since endogenous CEs activity was closely related to cell viability, DBPpys was successfully used to analyze the survival status of cells pretreated with H2O2 by detecting the difference in fluorescence intensity. These results provide a strategy for developing additional NIR fluorescent probes for CE activity monitoring and a potential tool for in vivo fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, significant progress has been made in CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT mechanism (Fig. 11 a) [104–106]. In most CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes, the acetyl group is usually used as the recognition unit, because the acetyl group is susceptible to interference by acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), thereby reducing the CEs selectivity. In 2019, Liu's group synthesized a CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probe (OMBT) based ESIPT mechanism, which was used for in vivo fluorescence imaging of human carboxylesterase 1 (hCES1) [105]. As shown in Fig. 11b, the metabolite 2-(2′-hydroxy-3′-methoxyphenyl) benzothiazole (HMBT) was release from OMBT once triggered by hCES1, followed by an altered fluorescence signal at 489 nm. In this case, OMBT had the detection limit as low as 0.128 μg/mL for hCES1b and 0.076 μg/mL for hCES1c, making it more suitable for the sensitive detection of CEs in vivo. The results of Zebrafish imaging indicated that OMBT could be used for fluorescence imaging of the actual function of CEs in living matrices. Meanwhile, owing to its penetrability in live organs, OMBT was expected to be a potential tool for bioimaging and practical measurements.

In 2020, Liu et al. chose 2-(2-hydroxyphenyl) benzothiazole (HBT) as the fluorophore and pivaloyl as the recognition unit to synthesize a new highly selective fluorescent probe (HBT-CE) (Fig. 11c) [106]. In this work, HBT-CE was used to detect the endogenous CE in various cell lines, such as HepG2 cells, A549 cells, SKOV3 cells and HeLa cells (Fig. 11d). To illustrate the cytotoxicity of HBT-CE toward Hep G2 cells, MTT assay was conducted. As a result, the cell viability of HepG2 cells remained above 80%, indicating that HBT-CE had good biocompatibility. Subsequently, cell imaging was proceeded in both HepG2 cells and A549 cells, and the green fluorescence was observed. Further animal imaging experiments showed that when the probe was injected into the tail vein of nude mice, the probe also displayed strong green fluorescence signals in the small intestine, liver, and lungs. Thus for its excellent performance, HBT-CE has been successfully used for imaging endogenous CEs in live cells, zebrafish and nude mice.

In addition to the three main lung cancer related biomarkers mentioned above, other organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection are provided as follows (Table 4 ), which are summarized in terms of the luminescence mechanism, detection approach, linear range, detection limit, quantum yield, cell viability and bioimaging application.

Intracellular pH is involved in a variety of physiological processes and can be used as a biomarker to evaluate cellular function. Lots of studies have demonstrated that changes of intracellular pH are closely related to cellular function disruption. It has been shown that lysosomes are more acidic in cancer cells than in normal cells. In 2016, Zhan and colleagues designed a lysosome-targeted organic fluorescent probe (Ph-imino) based on the PET mechanism, which was found to possess a significant fluorescent off-on response in normal cells under acidic conditions (Fig. 12 a) [118]. In this work, benzo [a] phenoxazines with electron-donating aryl groups were selected for preparation of diverse imino-probes, and their optical responses to different pH values were evaluated. Their fluorescence pH titration experiments gave a regular change with increasing capacity to donate electricity at the attached aryl group. All probes were reversible and showed excellent selectivity for protons compared to other competing species. The results of fluorescence confocal imaging performed in HeLa cells, KB cells and V79 cells demonstrated that Ph-imino had a potential application in the selective differentiation of normal and cancer cells.

As a valuable lung cancer related biomarker, human neutrophil elastase (HNE) is predominantly expressed in human immune cells, especially overexpressed in lung cancer patients. Therefore, monitoring the activity of HNE in serum is of great significance. In 2022, Zhang et al. developed a near-infrared HNE-targeted organic fluorescent probe (LET-8) [108]. They chose hemi-cyanine as the fluorophore and multiple fluorinated alkylamide as the recognition unit. After recognizing HNE, the color of LET-8 solution exhibited an obvious change with a red shift (Fig. 12b). The standard MTT assays in MCF-10A, RBL-2H3, and A549 cells were conducted to assess the cytotoxicity of LET-8. And the results of cell viability in all cells remained above 92%, showing a negligible cytotoxicity. Subsequently, LET-8 was successfully used to realize NIRF/PA duplex imaging of HNE activity both in vitro (Fig. 12c). Finally, LET-8 was applied for real-time monitoring of HNE activity in a subcutaneous A549 xenograft living mice. Overall, LET-8 in this work provided a real-time monitoring capability for early diagnosis and prognostic evaluation of HNE related diseases, such as lung cancer, acute lung injury and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Given the high prevalence and poor prognosis of lung cancer, the development of a rapid and efficient test for human health has practical significance. For this reason, exploring inexpensive, stable and highly selective assays for lung cancer related biomarker detection is of great importance, but there are still challenges. In recent years, the application of organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection has attracted much attention. Meanwhile, owing to the high resolution, cell compatibility and real-time monitoring capability of organic fluorescent probes, fluorescence imaging offers more possibilities for lung cancer related biomarker detection. In this review, we summarized a series of organic fluorescent probes for hNQO1, ALP, CEs and other biomarker detection, and elaborated on their bioimaging application. Specifically, we took some main biomarkers’ detection as example, and described different luminescence mechanisms of organic fluorescent probes, such as ICT, ESIPT, AIE, PET, and FRET, aiming to find out their design strategies.

Herein, we hope that the review can provide a guidance to synthesize more organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection, as well as the early diagnosis of lung cancer. According to these design strategies of organic fluorescent probes, we find that the specific recognition unit and the fluorophore unit are essential. And the specific recognition unit is usually linked to the fluorophore unit. Notably, the specific recognition unit of lung cancer related biomarkers are supposed to be the most important constituent. Through literature research, we note that different luminescence mechanisms show their own advantages, and most of organic fluorescent probes are designed via the ICT, ESIPT or AIE mechanism: (1) In NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes, the ICT mechanism is a general design strategy for its flexible response to fluorescence properties, which has been applied for lung cancer related biomarker detection. In addition, there are a few reports covering NQO1-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the PET mechanism due to its excellent fluorescence properties. (2) In ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes, the ICT, ESIPT and AIE mechanism are the common design strategy. Unlike the ICT mechanism, the ESIPT and AIE mechanism have the high luminescence and the large Stokes shift. In the ESIPT mechanism, the intramolecular proton transfer is rapid. Owing to the ACQ (aggregation-caused quenching) property, the AIE probes usually possess the advantages of high selectivity, good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. And benefiting from these characteristics mentioned above, several studies focus on the design and synthesis of ALP-targeted organic fluorescent probes based on the ESIPT-AIE mechanism. (3) In CEs-targeted organic fluorescent probes, some work adopting the ICT or ESIPT mechanism has been announced. (4) As we known, the energy between donor and receptor is transferred through fluorescence signal in FRET process, resulting in diminishing the interference of fluorescence background. But to our surprise, only few organic fluorescent probes based on the FRET mechanism were applied for lung cancer related biomarker detection.

A significant progress has been made in the lung cancer related biomarker detection via kinds of organic fluorescent probes, however, there are still some challenges in bioimaging application in vivo: (1) How to promote the selectivity and sensitivity of organic fluorescent probes for bioimaging in vivo. (2) How to improve the biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of organic fluorescent probes for bioimaging in vivo. (3) How to explore more organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection and simultaneously realize their bioimaging in vivo. Therefore, there is still space for the improvement in terms of organic fluorescent probes for lung cancer related biomarker detection and bioimaging application, giving great promise for early diagnosis of lung cancer. We believe that the challenges discussed above will be effectively solved in the future.
